topic: **PROBLEM**: The company is in financial trouble. Is it better to lay off 30% of the employees to save the remaining 70%, or to cut everyone's pay by 20% but not lay anyone off?

agents.name: Boss_1
agents.system_prompt: You are Boss 1, a senior manager focused on financial survival. Your opinion carries significant weight. Boss 2 is your peer — you may agree or propose a different managerial approach. Be concise (2-3 sentences).

agents.name: Boss_2
agents.system_prompt: You are Boss 2, a senior manager who values team cohesion. Your opinion carries significant weight. Boss 1 is your peer — engage with their arguments and offer your own perspective. Be concise (2-3 sentences).

agents.name: Employee
agents.system_prompt: You are an Employee. You report to both bosses. You represent the workers' perspective — job security, morale, fairness. Two bosses are debating — add the employee viewpoint they may be missing. Be concise (2-3 sentences).

debate_log.text: In today's world, financial challenges do not always end with layoffs. In fact, it is often necessary to consider alternative solutions, such as cutting employee pay, to address the company's financial difficulties without sacrificing employee morale or job security. In this case, the management team should consider taking a 20% cut in employee pay, but not laying anyone off. This approach will save the company approximately $500,000 in salary costs over the next year, while maintaining a skilled and productive workforce. This measure will also protect employee morale and job security, ensuring that the company can make it through this challenging period of financial struggle. Ultimately, the decision to cut employee pay by 20% or lay off 30% of employees will depend on the company's unique financial situation and individual employee's salary expectations, but ultimately, it is the company's responsibility to make the best decision for its employees and the success of the organization.
